paper.title: AI scientists produce results without reasoning scientifically
paper.claim: Across 8 domains and 25,000+ agent runs, LLM-based scientific agents fail to follow the epistemic norms of science: evidence is ignored in 68% of traces, refutation-driven belief revision occurs in only 26%, and convergent multi-test evidence is rare. The base model, not the agent scaffold, drives both performance and behavior.
paper.framing: Models keep improving. This will track how the paper's own agent benchmarks look as newer base models ship, on the paper's own metrics. It is not a verdict on the paper.
paper.readiness_caveat: Higher task scores don't imply scientific reasoning. The paper's finding is about epistemic behavior (using evidence, revising beliefs), a dimension separate from raw benchmark accuracy.
paper.status_note: Curated, not yet re-run. The pipeline (the open 'corral' harness plus Zenodo data) makes it reproducible, but reproduction is heavy: an agentic eval across 8 domains with a behavioral metric (evidence-ignored %), not a simple accuracy against a reference line. The plan is to re-run the base-model layer on today's frontier models.

claim_status.basis: Reproduction is queued. The agentic 'corral' harness is a heavier build, so this one goes live once we re-run it on current base models.

contributors.name: Ríos-García, Alampara, Jablonka et al.
contributors.role: Original benchmark of LLM scientific agents across 8 domains, and the open-source 'corral' harness plus Zenodo data.
